title: Vertical Agreements & RPM
content:
Session 4: Non-horizontal agreements and latest developments  
Maruti Suzuki [2021 | CCI] 
Facts:  
‚ùñ‚Äã Maruti Suzuki India Limited (MSIL), India‚Äôs largest passenger vehicle manufacturer, operates through a vast network of 
independent dealers across India. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Dealers purchase vehicles from MSIL, with title passing to the dealer, after which the dealer sells vehicles to end consumers. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Despite this transfer of title, MSIL implemented a Discount Control Policy which: 
‚û¢‚Äã Fixed maximum discounts / minimum resale prices for vehicles. 
‚û¢‚Äã Penalised dealers who sold vehicles below the prescribed price. 
‚ùñ‚Äã MSIL monitored compliance through: 
‚û¢‚Äã Internal email communications, 
‚û¢‚Äã Appointment of Mystery Shopping Agencies (MSAs), and 
‚û¢‚Äã Imposition of monetary penalties on erring dealers. 
‚ùñ‚Äã A particularly revealing aspect was the ‚ÄúSwati Kale incident‚Äù ‚Üí all penalty amounts collected from dealers were credited 
into the bank account of Swati Kale, the wife of a dealer principal.  
‚û¢‚Äã Upon examination, she admitted that she merely signed cheques and disbursed funds as instructed by MSIL, often 
for MSIL-related advertising expenses.  
‚û¢‚Äã This significantly weakened MSIL‚Äôs claim that the penalties were dealer-driven or independent. 
104 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Arguments of MSIL 
‚û¢‚Äã The Dealership Agreement permitted dealers to offer discounts at their discretion. 
‚û¢‚Äã Any discount alignment was dealer-to-dealer coordination, not manufacturer-driven. 
‚û¢‚Äã MSIL acted merely as a neutral third-party adjudicator, maintaining ‚Äúmarket discipline‚Äù and fairness. 
‚û¢‚Äã RPM had pro-competitive justifications, particularly in eliminating free-riding, as dealers might otherwise 
undercut prices while benefiting from MSIL‚Äôs investments in brand promotion and services. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether MSIL entered into an agreement with its dealers amounting to Resale Price Maintenance under Section 3(4)(e)? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether such RPM caused an Appreciable Adverse Effect on Competition (AAEC) in India under Section 3(1)? 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 3(4)(e) ‚Äì Vertical Restraints 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 2(b) ‚Äì Definition of ‚ÄúAgreement‚Äù 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Existence of an ‚ÄúAgreement‚Äù 
‚û¢‚Äã An agreement under Section 2(b) can exist outside the written dealership agreement. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Emails, monitoring mechanisms, and penalty enforcement demonstrated a clear meeting of minds. 
‚û¢‚Äã The argument that MSIL was merely a ‚Äúfather figure‚Äù or neutral regulator was untenable, as MSIL designed, 
monitored, and enforced the policy. 
‚û¢‚Äã Thus, an agreement amounting to RPM under Section 3(4)(e) was established. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Anti-Competitive Effects (AAEC Analysis) ‚Äì Section 19(3) 
‚û¢‚Äã Intra-Brand Competition 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Minimum RPM prevented dealers from competing with each other on price. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Price competition is a primary dimension of competition at the dealer level. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Suppression of intra-brand competition led to uniformly higher prices for consumers. 
‚û¢‚Äã Inter-Brand Competition 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Given MSIL‚Äôs significant market share (‚âà 51%), RPM had spillover effects. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Uniform pricing made MSIL‚Äôs prices easily observable, allowing competing OEMs to soften price 
competition. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
This reduced pricing pressure across the passenger vehicle market, harming consumers. 
‚û¢‚Äã Rejection of the Free-Rider Justification 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
MSIL argued that without RPM, dealers would cut costs on pre-sales services to offer lower prices, 
effectively "free-riding" on the investments made by other dealers or the manufacturer. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
MSIL already imposed detailed SOPs and SPGs governing dealer conduct and service quality + 
compliance was closely monitored and penalised, leaving little scope for free-riding. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Eliminating price competition does not necessarily incentivise better services or consumer welfare. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Even assuming marginal service improvements, the harm to competition outweighed any benefits. 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Commission held that MSIL entered into, monitored, and enforced a Discount Control Policy amounting to Resale 
Price Maintenance, resulting in AAEC in India, in violation of Section 3(4)(e) read with Section 3(1) of the Competition 
Act, 2002. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Penalty of ‚Çπ200 crores on MSIL. 
‚û¢‚Äã This was significantly below the statutory maximum (10% of average turnover), considering the post-pandemic 
recovery phase of the automobile sector. 
‚û¢‚Äã Notably, no penalty was imposed on directors under Section 48, and the order contains no detailed analysis on 
individual liability. 
 
105 
 
PF Digital Media Services Limited v UFO Moviez India Limited [2025 | CCI] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Information by cinematograph theatre owners (CTOs) and exhibitors operating cinema screens across multiple cities in 
India ‚Üí alleged anti-competitive conduct by UFO Moviez India Ltd., Scrabble Digital Ltd., and Qube Cinema Technologies 
Pvt. Ltd., all of whom were engaged in the business of supplying Digital Cinema Equipment (DCE) and Digital Cinema 
Services (DCS) in India. 
‚û¢‚Äã The allegations related to the functioning of two closely connected markets: the market for Digital Cinema 
Equipment, which includes projectors, servers, Theatre Management Systems (TMS), Cinema Management 
Systems (CMS), storage devices, and encryption systems; and the market for Digital Cinema Services, which 
includes content delivery, generation of encryption keys (KDMs), content mastering, satellite transmission, and 
quality assurance.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
As explained in the DG‚Äôs report, the deployment of digital cinema equipment is capital-intensive, and 
CTOs typically enter into long-term contracts in order to amortise the high upfront costs, coupled with 
recurring weekly or monthly service charges. 
‚û¢‚Äã Alleged that the OPs structured their contracts and technological systems in a manner that locked CTOs into 
exclusive and high-dependency arrangements. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Once DCE was installed, CTOs were compelled to obtain DCS from the same OP, with the format and 
encryption of content being entirely dependent on that service provider ‚Üí imposed unreasonable 
restraints, limited choice in content suppliers, and enabled refusal to deal with non-aligned integrators. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The installation of DCE locked theatres into long-term contracts that restricted their ability to source 
content from alternative providers. CTOs were subjected to multiple categories of charges, including 
upfront installation fees, recurring usage charges, and Virtual Print Fees (VPF), which cumulatively 
placed them at a competitive disadvantage. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The OPs were also accused of using proprietary technologies and access controls that prevented 
interoperability, thereby creating a state of technical and contractual dependence. 
‚û¢‚Äã OPs refused to ingest content or denied Key Delivery Messages (KDMs) when CTOs attempted to procure content 
from rival integrators or alternative pipelines.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The denial of KDMs, which are essential to unlock and play encrypted digital content, was alleged to 
amount to refusal to deal.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Artificial switching costs were created through proprietary software, long lock-in periods, and penalties 
for early termination, ensuring that CTOs remained dependent on the OPs.  
‚û¢‚Äã OP-2 and OP-3 acted in synchronisation with OP-1 and that the three entities had effectively formed a triopoly, 
exercising significant market power in the digital cinema ecosystem. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the conduct of the OPs amounted to tie-in arrangements under Section 3(4)(a), exclusive supply agreements under 
Section 3(4)(b), and refusal to deal under Section 3(4)(d) of the Competition Act, 2002, and 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether such conduct resulted in AAEC in the relevant markets for DCE and DCS. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 3(4) of the Competition Act prohibits vertical agreements that cause or are likely to cause AAEC, including tie-in 
arrangements, exclusive supply agreements, and refusal to deal.  
‚û¢‚Äã A tie-in exists where the purchase of one product (the tying product) is made conditional upon the purchase of 
another distinct product (the tied product).  
‚û¢‚Äã Exclusive supply arrangements arise where purchasers are restricted from obtaining goods or services from 
competing suppliers.  
‚û¢‚Äã Refusal to deal covers practices that restrict or deny market access to competitors or customers without objective 
justification.  
106 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã The assessment under Section 3(4) requires examination of market power, foreclosure effects, entry barriers, and consumer 
harm. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã DG‚Äôs Investigation and Findings 
‚û¢‚Äã DG found that OP-1, OP-2, and OP-3 had entered into substantially similar agreements with CTOs concerning 
the installation of digital cinema equipment and the provision of digital cinema services.  
‚û¢‚Äã These agreements required CTOs to accept DCE installation from the OP and to avail DCS from the same OP, 
thereby creating a linkage between the two.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The contracts typically involved initial installation charges and recurring weekly or monthly fees, which 
together constituted a bundled supply of equipment and services. 
‚û¢‚Äã Several agreements expressly prohibited CTOs from modifying, replacing, or altering the installed DCE and 
stipulated that the equipment could only be used for receiving content through the OP‚Äôs designated pipeline.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Even where interoperability was claimed, the DG noted that practical interoperability required technical 
permissions and processes that were controlled by the OPs themselves.  
‚óè‚Äã
Thus, theoretical compatibility was rendered ineffective by OP-controlled encryption systems 
and certification processes. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Since KDMs required to play content could only be generated by the OPs, CTOs were rendered 
substantially dependent on them.  
‚óè‚Äã
Technical and contractual conditions together prevented seamless switching between service 
providers. 
‚û¢‚Äã Most agreements were for periods of five years or more, often with automatic renewal clauses.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Exit attracted significant penalties, making switching economically unviable ‚Üí long-term lock-ins 
combined with exit penalties created substantial barriers to switching and limited market contestability. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Evidence from CTOs and Content Owners 
‚û¢‚Äã Many CTOs stated that once DCE was installed, they were compelled to use only the DCS of the same OP and that 
OPs refused to ingest content sourced through rival pipelines.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
CTOs repeatedly stated that the cost of removing and replacing DCE was prohibitive and that OPs 
refused to provide KDMs if content was sourced from competing integrators.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Depositions from major film producers and distributors such as Yash Raj Films, Reliance, Viacom18, Zee, and AA Films.  
‚û¢‚Äã Confirmed that content delivery and KDM generation were constrained by the DCE installed at theatre premises, 
thereby corroborating CTO complaints. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Even content owners were constrained by OP-controlled infrastructure, demonstrating the market power 
exercised by OPs. 
‚ùñ‚Äã CCI‚Äôs Assessment 
‚û¢‚Äã Concurred with the DG‚Äôs findings and conducted a detailed analysis of market dependence, noting that the digital 
transition in Indian cinema had rendered CTOs functionally dependent on DCE and DCS providers.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Long-term contracts, proprietary encryption systems, and refusal to generate KDMs created strong 
structural dependencies and deprived CTOs of meaningful choice. 
‚û¢‚Äã In its analysis of tie-in arrangements under Section 3(4)(a), the Commission identified DCE as the tying product 
and DCS as the tied product.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Although the agreements did not always expressly mandate exclusivity, technical and procedural 
restrictions imposed by OPs operated as a de facto tie-in.  
‚óè‚Äã
Since KDMs could only be generated by the OP that installed the equipment, CTOs were not 
free to avail DCS from alternative providers ‚Üí amounted to a tie-in arrangement within the 
meaning of Section 3(4)(a). 
‚û¢‚Äã With respect to exclusive supply agreements under Section 3(4)(b) 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The agreements, read together with the technical architecture and communication records, effectively 
prevented CTOs from availing DCS from any other provider.  
107 
 
‚óè‚Äã
High exit penalties, reconfiguration costs, and dependence on OP-controlled encryption systems 
foreclosed competing DCS providers. 
‚û¢‚Äã On refusal to deal under Section 3(4)(d), the Commission relied on multiple instances where OPs denied or 
withheld KDMs for content delivered through rival pipelines.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
While OPs cited technical incompatibilities and security concerns, the Commission found that these 
justifications were selectively invoked and lacked credibility.  
‚óè‚Äã
The denial of KDMs was held to be commercially motivated and aimed at protecting OPs‚Äô own 
DCS offerings. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The conduct resulted in AAEC by foreclosing competition in the DCS market, raising entry barriers, 
increasing switching costs, and reducing choice for CTOs and consumers. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Rejection of OP Defences 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission rejected the OPs‚Äô defences that CTOs voluntarily entered contracts, that interoperability existed, 
and that refusal of KDMs was justified on security grounds.  
‚û¢‚Äã The evidence demonstrated that CTOs lacked real bargaining power and that purported interoperability was 
illusory.  
‚û¢‚Äã The OPs also failed to demonstrate any pro-competitive justification for the restrictions imposed. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã UFO Moviez, Scrabble Digital, and Qube Cinema Technologies had imposed tie-in arrangements, exclusive supply 
obligations, and refusal to deal, in contravention of Section 3(4)(a), (b), and (d) read with Section 3(1) of the Competition 
Act.  
‚ùñ‚Äã The conduct was found to foreclose the market, restrict CTO choice, and limit competition in the digital cinema services 
sector.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Exercising its powers under Section 27(b), and applying the Penalty Guidelines, 2024, the Commission imposed monetary 
penalties proportionate to the nature, gravity, and duration of the contravention. 
 
 
Shamsher Kataria v Honda [2014 | CCI] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The informant alleged that automobile manufacturers (OEMs): 
‚û¢‚Äã Did not make genuine spare parts freely available in the open market. 
‚û¢‚Äã Restricted supply of spare parts only to authorised dealers and service centres. 
‚û¢‚Äã Controlled and regulated authorised workshops, thereby monopolising: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Sale of spare parts 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
After-sales repair and maintenance services 
‚û¢‚Äã Denied access to technical information, including: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Diagnostic tools 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Software programs 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Repair manuals and fault codes 
‚ùñ‚Äã This made it impossible for independent repairers to service modern automobiles. 
‚û¢‚Äã Restricted practices were nationwide, not localised. 
‚ùñ‚Äã There existed a huge price differential between: 
‚û¢‚Äã OEM procurement price from suppliers (OESs), and 
‚û¢‚Äã Price charged to end consumers (Mark-ups ranged from 100% to 5000%). 
‚ùñ‚Äã These practices: 
‚û¢‚Äã Created a monopoly in the secondary market, 
‚û¢‚Äã Raised vehicle ownership costs, 
‚û¢‚Äã Harmed consumers, independent repairers, and the economy. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The primary anti-competitive effect was foreclosure of independent service providers, through: 
108 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Entry barriers 
‚û¢‚Äã Denial of market access 
‚û¢‚Äã Consumer lock-in 
‚û¢‚Äã All relevant Section 19(3) factors. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the scope of investigation be expanded outside the information alleged? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the Opposite Parties violated Section 4 of the Competition Act by abusing their dominant position in the 
aftermarket for spare parts and repair services? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the Opposite Parties violated Section 3(4) by entering into anti-competitive vertical agreements restricting spare 
parts supply and repair services? 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 3(4): Vertical restraints (exclusive distribution, refusal to deal, tie-in) 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 4(2): Abuse of dominance: 
‚û¢‚Äã 4(2)(a)(ii): Unfair/excessive pricing 
‚û¢‚Äã 4(2)(c): Denial of market access 
‚û¢‚Äã 4(2)(e): Leveraging dominance into another market 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 19(3): AAEC factors 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 19(4): Dominance factors 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 3(5): IPR exemption (claimed, but rejected) 
 
Application: 
1.‚Äã
Identified three separate relevant markets‚Äî(i) manufacture and sale of cars (primary market), (ii) sale of spare parts and 
diagnostic tools, and (iii) repair and maintenance services (aftermarkets).  
a.‚Äã
It rejected the argument of a unified ‚Äúsystems market‚Äù, noting that Indian car owners do not engage in whole-life 
costing due to lack of information and high switching costs.  
i.‚Äã
Once a car is purchased, depreciation and resale losses make switching to another brand costly, preventing 
effective discipline of aftermarket conduct through the primary market. 
b.‚Äã
The Commission clarified that relevant market definition is a means to assess dominance, not an end in itself.  
i.‚Äã
Where dominance is felt in the aftermarket, that market must be examined separately. 
c.‚Äã
On spare parts, the Commission rejected OEMs‚Äô claims that each part constitutes a separate market ‚Üí automobile 
aftermarkets are cluster markets, where consumers demand a bundle of complementary goods and services to repair 
and maintain their car.  
i.‚Äã
Although individual parts are not interchangeable, consumers perceive and demand them as an 
aggregated bundle necessary to make the car functional.  
ii.‚Äã
Accordingly, substitutability is assessed at the level of the bundle, not individual components, making the 
aggregated aftermarket the relevant product market. 
2.‚Äã
Section 3(4) ‚Äì vertical restraints 
a.‚Äã
Existence of an ‚ÄúAgreement‚Äù 
i.‚Äã
The OEMs argued that: 
1.‚Äã
There was no agreement restricting competition, 
2.‚Äã
Supply restrictions were merely business decisions, and 
3.‚Äã
Independent repairers were not contractually prohibited from operating. 
ii.‚Äã
CCI rejected this argument, applying the competition-law conception of agreement, which is far wider 
than contract law. 
1.‚Äã
The DG found written agreements / letters of intent between: 
a.‚Äã
OEMs and Original Equipment Suppliers (OESs), and 
b.‚Äã
OEMs and authorised dealers. 
109 
 
2.‚Äã
These agreements contained clauses: 
a.‚Äã
Prohibiting OESs from supplying spare parts directly in the aftermarket, 
b.‚Äã
Requiring prior written consent of OEMs for third-party supply. 
iii.‚Äã
Additionally, overseas suppliers (OSSs)‚Äîoften group or parent companies‚Äîwere found to be supplying 
exclusively to OEMs, indicating tacit or unwritten arrangements. 
iv.‚Äã
Under Section 2(b), even informal, tacit, or non-enforceable understandings constitute agreements. 
1.‚Äã
Thus, the requirement of an agreement under Section 3(4) was clearly satisfied. 
3.‚Äã
Nature of the Vertical Restraints ‚Üí the conduct of OEMs fell squarely within multiple forms of vertical restraints 
recognised under Section 3(4): 
a.‚Äã
Exclusive Distribution (s.3(4)(c)) 
i.‚Äã
OESs were contractually restricted from selling spare parts to: 
1.‚Äã
Independent repairers, or 
2.‚Äã
Open market distributors. 
3.‚Äã
Spare parts could be sold only through OEM-authorised channels. 
ii.‚Äã
This exclusive funneling of supply eliminated alternative distribution channels. 
b.‚Äã
Refusal to Deal (s.3(4)(d)) 
i.‚Äã
OEMs refused to supply genuine spare parts, diagnostic tools, technical manuals and fault codes to 
independent repairers. 
ii.‚Äã
Without these inputs, independent workshops were functionally excluded from the market. 
iii.‚Äã
This constituted a constructive refusal to deal, even if not an explicit refusal. 
c.‚Äã
Appreciable Adverse Effect on Competition (AAEC) 
i.‚Äã
Since Section 3(4) carries no presumption of AAEC, the Commission applied Section 19(3) factors in 
detail. 
ii.‚Äã
Creation of Entry Barriers (s.19(3)(c)) 
1.‚Äã
OEMs were the sole source of genuine spare parts and diagnostic tools. 
2.‚Äã
Independent repairers: 
a.‚Äã
Could not procure parts, 
b.‚Äã
Could not access software, 
c.‚Äã
Could not legally service vehicles. 
3.‚Äã
This resulted in absolute foreclosure of the aftermarket to non-authorised players. 
iii.‚Äã
Foreclosure of Competition (s.19(3)(b)) 
1.‚Äã
The restrictions ensured that: 
a.‚Äã
Only authorised dealers could operate effectively. 
b.‚Äã
Independent service providers were systematically excluded, not because of inefficiency, 
but because of input denial. 
2.‚Äã
This is a classic case of input foreclosure in a vertically related market. 
iv.‚Äã
Consumer Harm (s.19(3)(a)) 
1.‚Äã
Consumers were denied: 
a.‚Äã
Choice of repairer, 
b.‚Äã
Competitive pricing, 
c.‚Äã
Geographic access (authorised dealers concentrated in cities). 
2.‚Äã
Mandatory warranty conditions forced consumers to: 
a.‚Äã
Use authorised dealers or 
b.‚Äã
Lose warranty protection. 
3.‚Äã
Thus, consumer autonomy and welfare were directly harmed. 
v.‚Äã
Price Effects (s.19(3)(a)) 
1.‚Äã
Mark-ups of 100% on average, and 
2.‚Äã
In some cases up to 5000% between: 
a.‚Äã
OEM procurement price from OESs, and 
110 
 
b.‚Äã
Price charged to consumers. 
3.‚Äã
Such mark-ups were possible only because competitive constraints were eliminated. 
vi.‚Äã
Rejection of Pro-Competitive Justifications 
1.‚Äã
OEMs argued: 
a.‚Äã
Restrictions ensured quality and safety, 
b.‚Äã
Prevented counterfeit spare parts, and 
c.‚Äã
Protected consumers. 
2.‚Äã
CCI‚Äôs analysis: 
a.‚Äã
Consumer choice is fundamental‚Äîquality concerns cannot justify foreclosure. 
b.‚Äã
If quality were the real concern: 
i.‚Äã
OEMs could certify independent repairers, 
ii.‚Äã
Or publish technical standards. 
iii.‚Äã
The existence of a black market was held to be a direct consequence of OEM 
restrictions, not a justification for them. 
4.‚Äã
Rejection of IPR Defence (s.3(5)) 
a.‚Äã
OEMs claimed the restraints were necessary to protect IP. 
b.‚Äã
CCI applied a strict ‚Äúnecessity‚Äù test: 
i.‚Äã
OEMs failed to prove valid IP ownership. 
ii.‚Äã
Foreign patents were irrelevant (territoriality principle). 
iii.‚Äã
Spare parts were governed by the Designs Act, not copyright. 
iv.‚Äã
Even assuming IP existed, selling finished spare parts does not compromise IP. 
c.‚Äã
Restrictions were far broader than necessary ‚Üí Section 3(5) exemption was unavailable. 
5.‚Äã
Section 4: Abuse of Dominance 
a.‚Äã
Dominance in the Relevant Market 
i.‚Äã
Relevant Market (Applied) 
1.‚Äã
Each OEM constituted a separate relevant market in: 
a.‚Äã
Sale of spare parts for its brand, 
b.‚Äã
Diagnostic tools, 
c.‚Äã
Repair and maintenance services. 
2.‚Äã
Due to: 
a.‚Äã
Brand-specific incompatibility, 
b.‚Äã
High switching costs, 
c.‚Äã
Consumer lock-in, 
ii.‚Äã
Each OEM enjoyed 100% market power in its aftermarket. 
b.‚Äã
Application of Section 19(4) Factors 
i.‚Äã
Market Share ‚Äì OEMs had complete control over genuine spare parts. 
ii.‚Äã
Dependence of Consumers ‚Äì they could not: 
1.‚Äã
Switch brands cheaply, 
2.‚Äã
Repair cars without OEM inputs. 
iii.‚Äã
Entry Barriers 
1.‚Äã
Technical barriers (software, tools), 
2.‚Äã
Contractual barriers (exclusive supply), 
3.‚Äã
Warranty-based barriers. 
4.‚Äã
Thus, dominance was unequivocally established. 
c.‚Äã
Abuse of Dominance 
i.‚Äã
Denial of Market Access ‚Äî s.4(2)(c) 
1.‚Äã
Independent repairers were denied spare parts, diagnostic tools, and repair manuals. 
2.‚Äã
This made effective competition impossible, not merely difficult. 
3.‚Äã
The denial was deliberate, systemic, and without objective justification. 
111 
 
4.‚Äã
Hence, Section 4(2)(c) was violated. 
ii.‚Äã
Unfair / Excessive Pricing ‚Äî s.4(2)(a)(ii) ‚Äì Economic Value Test 
1.‚Äã
The Commission applied the unfairness standard: a price is unfair if it bears no reasonable 
relation to economic value. 
a.‚Äã
Spare parts sourced cheaply from OESs, 
b.‚Äã
Sold at exponentially higher prices, 
c.‚Äã
Without corresponding value addition. 
2.‚Äã
While profit margins alone are not illegal, disproportionate mark-ups enabled by dominance 
constitute abuse. 
iii.‚Äã
Leveraging ‚Äî s.4(2)(e) 
1.‚Äã
OEMs leveraged dominance from Market A: Spare parts & diagnostic tools into Market B: 
Repair and maintenance services. 
2.‚Äã
They did so by: 
a.‚Äã
Restricting parts supply, 
b.‚Äã
Denying tools and manuals, 
c.‚Äã
Structuring warranties to penalise independent repairs. 
3.‚Äã
This forced consumers into authorised service networks, unfairly extending dominance into 
adjacent markets. 
d.‚Äã
No Justification or Exception under Section 4 
i.‚Äã
Unlike Section 3(5), Section 4 provides no IPR exemption. 
ii.‚Äã
Once dominance and abuse were shown: 
1.‚Äã
OEM intent was irrelevant, 
2.‚Äã
Consumer safety arguments failed, 
iii.‚Äã
Efficiency justifications were immaterial. 
 
In re Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Association of India, MakeMyTrip Pvt Ltd, Ibibo and  OYO [2022 | CCI] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The informant, Federation of Hotel and Restaurant Associations of India (FHRAI), filed information against MMT-Go 
and OYO alleging violations of Sections 3 and 4. 
‚ùñ‚Äã MMT-Go (OP-1) operates India‚Äôs largest Online Travel Agency (OTA), providing online intermediation services for 
booking hotels, flights, buses, etc.  
‚û¢‚Äã It is part of the MakeMyTrip Group, incorporated in Mauritius. 
‚ùñ‚Äã OYO (OP-3) operates as a budget hotel aggregator/franchise model, co-branding hotels under ‚ÄúOYO Rooms‚Äù and listing 
them across OTAs including MMT-Go. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Core Allegations: 
‚û¢‚Äã Confidential commercial agreement between MMT-Go and OYO granting OYO preferential treatment. 
‚û¢‚Äã Delisting / denial of market access to competing budget hotel chains such as Treebo and FabHotels. 
‚û¢‚Äã Wide price parity and room availability parity clauses imposed by MMT-Go on hotel partners. 
‚û¢‚Äã Deep discounting and alleged predatory pricing by MMT-Go. 
‚û¢‚Äã Excessive commission rates (22%‚Äì40%) charged by MMT-Go from standalone/budget hotels. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Procedural History: 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI found a prima facie case under s.3(4)(d) (refusal to deal) and s.4 (abuse of dominance). 
‚û¢‚Äã DG investigation ordered. 
‚û¢‚Äã Interim relief sought by Treebo and FabHotels under s.33 ‚Üí CCI ordered relisting. 
‚û¢‚Äã Gujarat HC set aside an interim order, but parties later settled relisting. 
‚û¢‚Äã DG confirmed violations under s.3(4) and s.4. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Final adjudication by CCI followed. 
 
Issues: 
112 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the agreement between MMT-Go and OYO constituted an anti-competitive vertical agreement under s.3(4)(d) 
read with s.3(1). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether MMT-Go was dominant in the relevant market under s.4. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether price parity, room parity, exclusivity, and deep discounting amounted to abuse of dominance under s.4(2)(a)(i) and 
s.4(2)(c). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the delisting of Treebo and FabHotels amounted to denial of market access. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether deep discounting amounted to predatory pricing. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether interim relief under s.33 was justified. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Appropriate penalty methodology in platform markets (Excel Crop Care issue). 
 
Rule: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 3(1) ‚Äì Anti-competitive agreements. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 3(4)(d) ‚Äì Refusal to deal (vertical restraints). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 4(1) ‚Äì Abuse of dominant position. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 4(2)(a)(i) ‚Äì Unfair pricing. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 4(2)(c) ‚Äì Denial of market access. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 19(3) ‚Äì AAEC factors. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 19(4) ‚Äì Dominance factors. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 33 ‚Äì Interim measures. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 27(b) ‚Äì Penalty. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Excel Crop Care ‚Äì Relevant turnover principle. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Relevant Market Analysis (Foundational To S.4) 
‚û¢‚Äã Correct Point of Assessment: Hotel-Side Perspective 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Commission begins by correcting the starting point of market definition. Since the alleged conduct 
concerns imposition of contractual restraints on hotels and franchise service providers, the relevant 
market must be assessed from the standpoint of hotels, not end-consumers. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
This is critical because: 
‚óè‚Äã
The same distribution channel may be substitutable for consumers but not substitutable for 
suppliers. 
‚óè‚Äã
In platform markets, asymmetric dependence is common: hotels depend more on OTAs than 
consumers depend on any single OTA. 
‚óè‚Äã
Failing to identify the correct user group would distort substitutability analysis. 
‚û¢‚Äã Online vs Offline: Why They Are Not Substitutes 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
MMT-Go argued that: 
‚óè‚Äã
Consumers freely switch between online booking, walk-ins, travel agents, taxi drivers, etc. 
‚óè‚Äã
Therefore, online and offline belong to the same market. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Commission rejects this at multiple levels: 
‚óè‚Äã
Functional Differences ‚Äì OTAs provide: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Discoverability 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Algorithmic ranking 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Price comparison 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Real-time inventory management 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Offline channels lack these features. 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Thus, characteristics and intended use differ, failing the substitutability test. 
‚óè‚Äã
Consumer Segmentation ‚Äì Consumers using online booking: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Pre-plan, 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Compare prices, 
113 
 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Use digital payments. 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Consumers relying on offline channels: 
‚û¢‚Äã Decide post-arrival, 
‚û¢‚Äã Depend on intermediaries, 
‚û¢‚Äã Are not the same cohort. 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Even if some consumers use both channels, they do not use them interchangeably for 
the same booking. 
‚óè‚Äã
Multi-homing ‚âÝ Substitution 
‚óÜ‚Äã
The Commission clarifies an important principle: 
‚û¢‚Äã Multi-homing only shows usage, not substitutability. 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Hotels using offline channels during high demand periods: 
‚û¢‚Äã Prefer them due to higher margins, 
‚û¢‚Äã Use OTAs to fill unsold inventory. 
‚û¢‚Äã This indicates complementarity, not substitutability. 
‚óè‚Äã
Conclusion: Offline booking does not constrain MMT-Go‚Äôs market power. 
‚û¢‚Äã OTAs as a Distinct Online Market 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Within online channels, the Commission further narrows the market to: Online intermediation services 
for booking hotels. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
This excludes: 
‚óè‚Äã
Meta-search engines (Google, Trivago), 
‚óè‚Äã
Direct hotel websites, 
‚óè‚Äã
Corporate bookings. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Why Meta-search Engines Are Not Substitutes 
‚óè‚Äã
They do not consummate transactions. 
‚óè‚Äã
They merely redirect traffic. 
‚óè‚Äã
Budget hotels cannot rely on paid search visibility. 
‚óè‚Äã
OTAs bundle visibility with conversion. 
‚óè‚Äã
Presence on Google ‚âÝ visibility on an OTA. 
‚û¢‚Äã Two-Sided Platform Economics 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Commission explicitly applies platform theory: 
‚óè‚Äã
MMT-Go operates a two-sided platform: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Side 1: Hotels 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Side 2: Consumers 
‚óè‚Äã
Presence of positive indirect network effects: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
More hotels ‚Üí more consumers 
‚óÜ‚Äã
More consumers ‚Üí greater hotel dependence 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Crucially: 
‚óè‚Äã
Substitutability analysis on one side cannot be transposed to the other. 
‚óè‚Äã
Even if consumers are multi-home, hotels cannot afford to ignore a dominant OTA. 
‚óè‚Äã
This cements OTAs as a separate, narrow relevant market. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Dominance Of Mmt-Go (S.19(4) Analysis) 
‚û¢‚Äã Market Share: Stayed Room Nights (RN) 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Commission endorses Stayed Room Nights as the most appropriate metric because: 
‚óè‚Äã
It reflects actual transactions, not listed inventory or app downloads. 
‚óè‚Äã
It captures network strength, which is central in platform markets. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Commission rejects revenue-based metrics because: 
‚óè‚Äã
Price per room varies widely. 
‚óè‚Äã
Equal revenue may mask radically different user engagement. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
MMT-Go‚Äôs RN share (~63%) consistently exceeded competitors like Booking.com and Cleartrip. 
114 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Network Effects as a Source of Market Power 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Commission gives significant weight to network effects: 
‚óè‚Äã
Over 45,000 properties listed. 
‚óè‚Äã
Greater choice ‚Üí consumer preference. 
‚óè‚Äã
Consumer preference ‚Üí hotel dependence. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
This creates a self-reinforcing cycle: 
‚óè‚Äã
Dominance today strengthens dominance tomorrow. 
‚óè‚Äã
Such effects are particularly durable and difficult to dislodge, even with entry. 
‚û¢‚Äã Entry Barriers 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Despite rapid market growth: 
‚óè‚Äã
Few successful new entrants. 
‚óè‚Äã
Foreign platforms failed to scale. 
‚óè‚Äã
Small domestic players stagnated. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Reasons: 
‚óè‚Äã
High customer acquisition costs, 
‚óè‚Äã
Commission-funded discount wars, 
‚óè‚Äã
Data advantages, 
‚óè‚Äã
Brand recall, 
‚óè‚Äã
Tie-up with OYO. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Commission finds structural barriers, not merely strategic ones. 
‚û¢‚Äã Conclusion on Dominance 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Considering: 
‚óè‚Äã
Market share, 
‚óè‚Äã
Network effects, 
‚óè‚Äã
Entry barriers, 
‚óè‚Äã
Dependence asymmetry, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
MMT-Go is found to possess the ability to operate independently of competitive forces, satisfying s.4 
dominance. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Abuse Of Dominance 
‚û¢‚Äã Parity Obligations (PRICE + ROOM) [s.4(2)(a)(i) + s.4(2)(c)] 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Nature of the Clauses 
‚óè‚Äã
MMT-Go imposed: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Narrow Parity ‚Üí restricting lower prices on hotels‚Äô own websites [still justified to 
prevent free-riding] 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Wide Parity ‚Üí restricting lower prices on any other platform 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Room availability parity: Same inventory across platforms + coupled with exclusivity 
conditions. 
‚óÜ‚Äã
These were non-negotiable due to MMT-Go‚Äôs dominance. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Competitive Harm Analysis 
‚óè‚Äã
Suppression of Inter-Platform Competition 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Other OTAs cannot attract hotels by offering: 
‚û¢‚Äã Lower commissions, 
‚û¢‚Äã Better terms. 
‚û¢‚Äã Any cost reduction cannot be passed to consumers. 
‚óè‚Äã
Foreclosure of Entry 
‚óÜ‚Äã
New platforms cannot differentiate. 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Price becomes a non-competitive parameter. 
‚óè‚Äã
Reinforcement of Dependence 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Hotels receive most bookings via MMT-Go. 
115 
 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Leads to acceptance of higher commissions. 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Bargaining imbalance worsens. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Rejection of ‚ÄúIndustry Practice‚Äù Defence ‚Üí the Commission makes a dominance-sensitive assessment: 
‚óè‚Äã
Conduct that may be benign for a small player becomes harmful when done by a dominant 
gatekeeper. 
‚óè‚Äã
Other OTAs‚Äô parity clauses are not comparable due to: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Lower discounts, 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Smaller scale, 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Lack of network effects. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Free-Riding Justification Rejected (Wide MFN) ‚Äì the Commission draws a clear distinction: 
‚óè‚Äã
Narrow parity may address free-riding. 
‚óè‚Äã
Wide parity: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Eliminates competition, 
‚óÜ‚Äã
It is disproportionate. 
‚óè‚Äã
Further: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
OTA investments are not hotel-specific. 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Deep discounts already negate incentives to free-ride. 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Held: Wide parity obligations are abusive. 
‚û¢‚Äã Deep Discounting (Contextual Abuse) 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The Commission clarifies: 
‚óè‚Äã
Deep discounting per se is not illegal ‚Üí difference in deep discounts and predatory pricing ‚Üí  
‚óè‚Äã
However, in this case it: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Reinforced network effects, 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Was funded through high commissions, 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Worked alongside parity clauses. 
‚óè‚Äã
Result: 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Customers flocked to MMT-Go, 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Other OTAs could not compete, 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Long-term price competition weakened. 
‚óÜ‚Äã
Thus, discounting became exclusionary in effect, though not predatory. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Predatory Pricing (NOT ESTABLISHED) 
‚û¢‚Äã While DG found below-cost pricing, CCI rejects this due to: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Faulty cost classification, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Inadequate intent analysis, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Platform-specific economics. 
‚û¢‚Äã Loss-making alone is insufficient in digital markets where: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Scale precedes profitability. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
No predatory pricing violation found. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Denial Of Market Access (S.3(4)(D) + S.4(2)(C)) 
‚û¢‚Äã Vertical Agreement with OYO 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Despite disputes over franchising labels: 
‚óè‚Äã
OYO and MMT-Go are clearly in a vertical relationship. 
‚óè‚Äã
Preferential treatment granted to OYO. 
‚û¢‚Äã Delisting as Foreclosure 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Delisting Treebo and FabHotels excluded them from the dominant distribution channel. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Created: 
‚óè‚Äã
Artificial advantage for OYO, 
‚óè‚Äã
Disincentives for hotels to associate with rivals. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
This was: 
116 
 
‚óè‚Äã
Not based on efficiency, 
‚óè‚Äã
Not competition on merits, 
‚óè‚Äã
Directly exclusionary. 
‚ùñ‚Äã AAEC under s.19(3) ‚Äì the conduct: 
‚û¢‚Äã Denied market access, 
‚û¢‚Äã Raised entry barriers, 
‚û¢‚Äã Reduced choice, 
‚û¢‚Äã Foreclosed competition. 
‚û¢‚Äã Violates s.3(4)(d) and s.4(2)(c). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Interim Relief (S.33) 
‚û¢‚Äã CCI applied the three-prong test: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Prima facie case ‚Äì established. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Irreparable harm ‚Äì digital delisting causes immediate foreclosure. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Balance of convenience ‚Äì favoured relisting. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Penalty & Platform Turnover 
‚û¢‚Äã The Commission departs from strict Excel Crop Care logic: 
‚û¢‚Äã Platform verticals are interlinked. 
‚û¢‚Äã Hotel intermediation feeds into flights, ads, data. 
‚û¢‚Äã Hence: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Relevant turnover interpreted broadly. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
5% penalty imposed with mitigating factors.
cases: PF Digital Media Services Limited v UFO Moviez India Limited
key_principles: ‚Äã Maruti Suzuki India Limited (MSIL), India‚Äôs largest passenger vehicle manufacturer, operates through a vast network of
‚Äã Dealers purchase vehicles from MSIL, with title passing to the dealer, after which the dealer sells vehicles to end consumers.
‚Äã Despite this transfer of title, MSIL implemented a Discount Control Policy which:
‚Äã Fixed maximum discounts / minimum resale prices for vehicles.
‚Äã Penalised dealers who sold vehicles below the prescribed price.
‚Äã MSIL monitored compliance through:
‚Äã Internal email communications,
‚Äã Appointment of Mystery Shopping Agencies (MSAs), and
‚Äã Imposition of monetary penalties on erring dealers.
‚Äã A particularly revealing aspect was the ‚ÄúSwati Kale incident‚Äù ‚Üí all penalty amounts collected from dealers were credited
‚Äã Upon examination, she admitted that she merely signed cheques and disbursed funds as instructed by MSIL, often
‚Äã This significantly weakened MSIL‚Äôs claim that the penalties were dealer-driven or independent.
‚Äã The Dealership Agreement permitted dealers to offer discounts at their discretion.
‚Äã Any discount alignment was dealer-to-dealer coordination, not manufacturer-driven.
‚Äã MSIL acted merely as a neutral third-party adjudicator, maintaining ‚Äúmarket discipline‚Äù and fairness.
‚Äã RPM had pro-competitive justifications, particularly in eliminating free-riding, as dealers might otherwise
‚Äã Whether MSIL entered into an agreement with its dealers amounting to Resale Price Maintenance under Section 3(4)(e)?
‚Äã Whether such RPM caused an Appreciable Adverse Effect on Competition (AAEC) in India under Section 3(1)?
‚Äã Section 3(4)(e) ‚Äì Vertical Restraints
‚Äã Section 2(b) ‚Äì Definition of ‚ÄúAgreement‚Äù
